coredata.dc:title: Flexible electrospun nanofibrous film integrated with fluorescent carbon dots for smartphone-based detection and cellular imaging application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Amin, Niloufar
Afkhami, Abbas
Hosseinzadeh, Leila
Amin, Ali
Madrakian, Tayyebeh
coredata.dc:description: The dose of administered chemotherapy drugs is crucial to determine due to the potential for efficient or adverse outcomes for cancer patients. To date, no user-friendly and low-cost method of doxorubicin (DOX) detection using nontoxic and biodegradable materials has been reported. For this reason, in this work, we have developed for the first time a nanofiber-based sensing platform for sensitive and on-site DOX assay in just 10 min. This is obtained thanks to printable, porosity and embeddability features of electrospun nanofibrous films (ENFFs) combined with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) as sensing probes. The assay was done by just pipetting analyte on the hydrophilic spots of the fabricated photoluminescence water-stable ENFFs where the color intensity was being darkened. DOX quenched NS-CDs fluorescence onto ENFFs through inner filter effect. The developed sensor was either coupled with smartphone technology to provide miniaturized, portable and easy-to-use device or an ordinary spectrofluorimeter for solid-state sensing applications (detection limit of 5.4 nM). Moreover, applicability of the designed sensor was evaluated in human serum with satisfactory recoveries. It is more interesting that the fabricated NS-CDs/ENF scaffolds have a high potential to detect the intracellular DOX to enhance cell proliferation leading to be considered as a multimodal tool in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone
Electrospun nanofibrous film
Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Doxorubicin
Intracellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture, proliferation and cytotoxicity test
Synthesis and characterization of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/PVA-ENFFs
Analytical performance of the DOX sensing
In vitro cellular imaging and cytotoxicity assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Councils of Bu-Ali Sina University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study is financially supported by the Research Councils of Bu-Ali Sina University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of NS-CDs/ENFF nanohybride. (a)TEM image and histogram of size distribution of NS-CDs (inset); (b) TEM image of NS-CDs/ENFF; (c) SEM images of the NS-CDs/ENFF before and (d) after crosslinking with GA vapor; insets are their corresponding histogram of size distribution; (e-h) are elemental mapping images of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur, respectively. (i) Overlay image of all elements.

The assay responses toward DOX. (a) FL responses of the NS-CDs/ENFF toward various concentrations of DOX (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 25.0 µM); (b) The relationship between F0 /F and the DOX concentration. Inset is the magnification of calibration curve for DOX detection in lower ranges. (C) Typical images of the NS-CDs/ENFF in the presence of DOX captured by smartphone and (d) related calibration curve. (e) Fluorescence spectrum of NS-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of DOX from 0 to 100 µM and (f) related calibration curve. (F0 and F correspond to the FL intensities of the NS-CDs in the absence and presence of DOX, respectively). Error bars indicates the standard errors of three independent experiments.

(a) FL response of NS-CDs/DOX in the absence (pink column) and presence (black column) of different possible interfering species (Interfering species and DOX concentration were 150.0 and 15.0 μM). (b) Fluorescence images of the ENFFAD upon addition of different possible interfering substances (50.0 µM) under 365 nm UV lamp.

The mechanism of FL quenching of NS-CDs by DOX. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of DOX and the excitation and emission spectra of NS-CDs; (b) The time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) of NS-CDs alone and after adding of 50.0 µM DOX; Parameters used in the equation to correct for the IFE. (c) F0 refers to the excitation beam; FL refers to the observed FL beam; s is the thickness of excitation beam (0.10 cm); d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm) and g is the distance between the edge of excitation beam and the edge of cuvette (0.40 cm). Observed (Eobsd) and corrected (Ecor) quenching efficiency of NS-CDs towards DOX. E = 1 − F/F0, F0 and F are FL intensities of NS-CDs in the absence and presence of DOX, respectively.

(a) Cytotoxicity studies of fibroblast growth on (■) control, (■) NS-CDs, (■) PVA-ENF and (■) NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs. Bright field (b, d) and fluorescence (c, f) images of fibroblast cells treated with NS-CDs (b) and NS-CDs/PVA -ENFs (e), excitation wavelength: 408 nm.

Fluorescence microscope images of cells labeled with NS-CDs/PVA ENF scaffold before (second column) and after addition (third column) of 5.0 µM DOX with excitation wavelength of 405 nm (b, c), 488 nm (e, f) and bright field (a, d), respectively. The scale bar was 200 μm.
Schematic illustration of the fabrication ENFFAD (a, b); Sensing assay principle (c) Fluorescence-based assay (d) Smartphone-based device for quantitative evaluation; In vitro imaging (e).
Detection of DOX Spiked in serum Samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Flexible electrospun nanofibrous film integrated with fluorescent carbon dots for smartphone-based detection and cellular imaging application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, Health Technology Inistitute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, Health Technology Inistitute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nakh Shimi Azar Knowledge Based-Company, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nakh Shimi Azar Knowledge Based-Company, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dose of administered chemotherapy drugs is crucial to determine due to the potential for efficient or adverse outcomes for cancer patients. To date, no user-friendly and low-cost method of doxorubicin (DOX) detection using nontoxic and biodegradable materials has been reported. For this reason, in this work, we have developed for the first time a nanofiber-based sensing platform for sensitive and on-site DOX assay in just 10 min. This is obtained thanks to printable, porosity and embeddability features of electrospun nanofibrous films (ENFFs) combined with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) as sensing probes. The assay was done by just pipetting analyte on the hydrophilic spots of the fabricated photoluminescence water-stable ENFFs where the color intensity was being darkened. DOX quenched NS-CDs fluorescence onto ENFFs through inner filter effect. The developed sensor was either coupled with smartphone technology to provide miniaturized, portable and easy-to-use device or an ordinary spectrofluorimeter for solid-state sensing applications (detection limit of 5.4 nM). Moreover, applicability of the designed sensor was evaluated in human serum with satisfactory recoveries. It is more interesting that the fabricated NS-CDs/ENF scaffolds have a high potential to detect the intracellular DOX to enhance cell proliferation leading to be considered as a multimodal tool in biomedical research and clinical diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite great advances in cancer treatment, a major challenge still continues in the mid-late stage of chemotherapy associated with chemotherapeutic agents [1–3]. This is because anticancer drugs have narrow therapeutic index that, if not timely and precisely optimized their efficient doses and those can produce intoxication, lead to adverse damage to the normal tissues/cells, and resulting in treatment failure [2,4,5].

With the emergence of point-of-care testing (PoCT) to improve clinical setting and widely recorded needs for therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) as a criterion for evaluating personalized drug dosages [2,4,6], a need to develop a precise, robust and easy to use device for real time sensing is the want of the hour.

Nowadays fluorescent-based sensors have been recognized as efficient analytical tools in bioanalysis and bioimaging with regard to their simple visual detection, ease of operation and high sensitivity[7,8]. Clearly, this also conducted to the development of various fluorescent nanomaterials used as a probe. However, almost all of these sensors have been applied in solution state, significantly restricting their usage in the field applications due to poor stability over long periods, large reagent consumption and difficult to store and transport [9–11]. It is well known that one of the most momentous issue encountered in solution-based sensing is low fluorescence (FL) efficacy due to the self-quenching caused by the environmental changes[10,12].

Over the past few years, it is a favored to develop fluorescent solid state-based platforms consisting of attaching various kinds of fluorescent probe onto the solid support, transforming easily into a device format for day-to-day life requirements [10–14]. As a one kind of solid support-based sensors, paper-based sensors, or “lab-on-paper” devices have recently gained a great deal of interest for (bio) chemical sensing and diagnosis areas [13–15]. However, their applications are usually narrowed to optical sensing due to inborn features of paper like limited surface area, large surface roughness and low mechanical strength and flexibility[16].

Interestingly, some efforts have already been made to introduce film-based fluorescent sensors into the optical (bio) sensing area, offering the possibility to produce portable, low cost, being able to easy transportation and storing up platform for on-site applications [9,10]. Noteworthy, the performance of the film-based sensor is strongly depending on film's thickness such that the fabrication of porous films is highly desirable for enhancing analytes’ diffusion rate, and thus better sensitivity [17].

Compared to various methods for film production, electrospining has been considered as the most versatile technique to fabricate polymer nanofibrous films with desired size and thickness [9,18]. Moreover, electrospun nanofibers (ENFs) with a large specific surface area and nanoscale porous structure can be easily functionalized with sensing elements for improving their sensitivity and selectivity towards analytes [18,19]. Although sensors have already been designed whose FL nanoparticles (NPs) are integrated with ENFs for detecting biomolecules and environmentally relevant analytes requiring to readout instrument, serious toxicity or relatively low FL quantum yield, or costly preparation methods limited their application for in-field decision-making [18,20–24].

To tackle the limitations, special efforts have been directed towards integrating carbon dots (CDs) into ENFs to make an environmental-friendly fluorescent film particularly for optical sensing, biomedical tools and photocatalysis devices [25–27]. Take into account that carbon nanodots (CDs) represent a rising star of fluorescence probes due to their numerous merits, such as low cost in synthesis, high photostability, water solubility, high cell permeability and excellent biocompatibility[25,28,29]. Nevertheless, all of the reported studies are either not able of performing quantitative analysis or not yet basically applicable, to the best of our knowledge.

Accordingly, in this research for the first time, we exploited the fabricated electrospun nanofibrous film-based analytical device (ENFFAD) as a portable, user-friendly and affordable device, benefiting from the advantages of poly-vinyl alcohol (PVA) and the strategies of probing carbon dots co-doped with heteroatoms like nitrogen and sulfur (NS-CDs) led to reliable, sensitive and visual analysis. It maybe prominent notice here that the incorporation of carbon dots inside PVA-ENF as a solid matrix not only offers an efficient fluorescent sensory hybrid platform but also improves the stability and minimize aggregation of this FL NPs against environmental factors [11,21]. Besides, combining the platform with smartphones, as the most common portable detector, led to enhance the performance of ENFFAD device in terms of accessibility, miniaturizing and costs of the detecting [13,30].

As a proof of concept of our fabricated ENFFAD, doxorubicin (DOX), an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent used in oncology, was applied. Its serum levels have been correlated with therapeutic efficiency so that needs to be accurately monitored [31,32]. Its serum levels have been correlated with therapeutic efficiency so that needs to be accurately monitored [33]. Conventional monitoring methods of DOX levels in patients involve quantitative enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA)[34], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)-Tandem MS [35], capillary electrophoresis[36] and UV–vis spectrophotometry[37]. Yet ELIZA test requires costly bio-receptors and display false positives and lack of sensitivity. Other mentioned methods relied on complex manipulation and sophisticated equipment, which would be difficult to perform at remote locations. Therefore, an easy to use, rapid and low-cost way to sense DOX is highly demanded.

With all these backgrounds, blue-emitting NS-CDs were synthesized from citric acid and rubeanic acid as precursors. Further, NS-CDs/PVA-ENFFs were fabricated by direct doping highly FL NS-CDs as sensing elements inside NFs-matrix and then cross-linked chemically via Glutaraldehyde (GA) vapor to obtain a water-stable substrate. In this context, more accessibility as well as more direct interaction between embedded NS-CDs and DOX takes place thanks to porous nature and high surface area of PVA-ENFs, which affirms the superior sensing ability compared to solution state.

The quenched FL signal of the developed ENFFAD as analytical signal was monitored either visually by smart phone or spectroscopically by a solid-state spectrofluorimeter. The designed mobile based-sensor can be operated as a PoC device for outdoor application. Successful rapid and visual detection of DOX by NS-CDs/PVA-ENFF instigated us to further investigation in living normal cells. Human fibroblast cell line was chosen as the in vitro cell model since they show characteristics similar to fibroblasts, which can be recommended for evaluating skin tissue engineering scaffolds.

It was found that NS-CDs/PVA-ENFFs are highly biocompatible which can readily enter into cell cytoplasm and act as an enhancer in cell proliferation, thereby serving as a nucleus labelling agent and good diagnostic tool. Various researches have been reported on ENF scaffolds in cell culture and tissue engineering; however, no reported study exists for operation of CDs embedded in ENF scaffolds as FL imaging and sensing probe in living cells.

All commercial materials were of analytical grade and handled according to the material safety data sheets suggested by the suppliers. Poly vinyl alcohol (PVA, Mw = 72000 g mol−1, ≥98% hydrolyzed), Hydrochloric acid (37% purity), Rubeanic acid, Doxorubicin hydrochloride, Penicillin/Streptomycin, 3-(4, 5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution and Dulbecco’s modified Eagle's medium (DMEM-F12) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid, Glutaraldehyde (GA, 25% aqueous solution) and all of the used salts were procured from Merck, Germany. A Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4 buffer solution was used to maintain the pH of solutions at 8.0. A commercially available printer ink (HP 49A Black Original LaserJet Toner Cartridge; Q5949A) was used.

The FL spectra of NS-CDs solution and NS-CDs/ENFFs were obtained with a spectrofluorimeter (Model Perkin Elmer equipped with a Xenon arc lamp, slit width = 10 nm) and (Cary Eclipse equipped with fiber optics accessory (2 m long), Varian, USA), respectively. All FL spectra were collected at excitation wavelength 340.0 nm and emission wavelength 430 nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by using a diode array spectrophotometer (Model UV-8453, Agilent, USA); Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Perkin-Elmer model (Spectrum GX., USA). The steady-state PL spectra of NS-CDs /ENFFs was obtained using a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin-Yvon, France). All ENFFs were prepared using commercial full option electrospinning equipment (Lab2 ESI-I, Nanoazma, Iran). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were collected using a Model G2 F20 TWIN microscopy system equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis (FEI Tecnai, USA). Pictures were taken with an Apple iPhone 7 smartphone, in manual mode, autofocus; at ISO 100 and a shutter speed of 1/180 s.

In a typical synthesis, 4.5 mmol citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and 4.2 mmol rubeanic acid was introduced to the above solution under stirring for 15 min to get a homogeneous suspension. Then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated in the oven to 200 °C for 6 h. The dark brown solution was centrifuged for 20 min at 10000 rpm to remove large precipitates and then the supernatant dialyzed (cut-off: 3.0 kDa) against deionized water for 48 h to remove unreacted reagents. Finally, the resultant pale brown NS-CDs solution was stored in the dark at room temperature.

NS-CDs/ENFFs were prepared through a three-step procedure, containing preparation of solutions, electrospinning process and cross-linking treatment. Detailed synthetic procedures are presented in the SI, 2.1.

Ready-to-use and simple ENFFAD strip was fabricated as schematically shown in Scheme 1 b. Circular areas (diameter of 5 mm) on black color background defining the sensing zones were drawn using Microsoft Word 2016 and created by an inkjet printer. That is because the printer ink was penetrated the nanofiber texture, making hydrophobic walls on cross-linked NFFs. To do so, the desired layouts printed onto cross-linked NS-CDs/ENFF attached to normal paper (A4). After creating the sensing areas onto the NS-CDs/ENFF, it was detached from the A4 paper and then were cut into strips by scissor. Each strip would be used as an ENFFAD with 10 circular detection areas (white spots in normal light and blue spots under UV lamp) and hydrophobic area (black part). The fabricated ENFFAD were then stored in dark conditions at 4 °C before use.

The detection of DOX in both solution and solid state was carried out. In a typical procedure for spectrofluorimetric determination in solution state, 0.8 µL of serial dilutions from the stock solution of DOX was mixed with a 2.0 mL phosphate buffer (PB) solution (0.1 M, pH 8.0) containing as-synthesized NS-CDs. The solution was then thoroughly mixed, followed by measuring the FL intensity at 430.0 nm after 2.0 min.

For solid-state sensing, the assay procedure starts by adding 4.0 μL volume of PB solution (0.1 M, pH 8.0) containing different concentrations of DOX in the detection areas of ENFFAD strip and then allowed to dry in the air for 10 min. Next, NS-CDs/ENFFs strip was transferred into the fabricated homemade 3D-printed dark box. Either a fiber optic probe or a 5 mm LED of 20 mA at 365 nm wavelength as a UV source to excite embedded NS-CDs in ENFF were used. The former case records FL spectra to observe fluorescence change as analytical signal (Scheme 1c). In latter case, the pictures/FL intensities of the related sensing zones were taken with a smartphone Apple iPhone 7 camera and process by Image J software, following the protocol reported before [13,38]. Scheme a-d illustrates all the steps involved in the sensor design and its application.

Clinical applicability of the designed ENFFAD was confirmed by measuring different concentrations of DOX in serum samples from healthy volunteers at Besat hospital (Hamedan, Iran) with informed consent. The samples were pre-treated according to our previous protocol [8] and then assayed following the procedure for DOX detection in buffer solution (three replicates for each DOX concentration in each sample).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, proliferation and cytotoxicity test
ce:section.ce:para: Human fibroblast cell lines were cultured in DMEM-F12 containing 10% FBS, 100 U/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin at 37 °C, 5% CO2 and humid environment and let them to reach maximum growth. Cell viabilities and proliferation of the ENF scaffolds (NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs and PVA-ENFs) against fibroblast cells were determined by MTT assay. The essential procedure details of cell culture and MTT assay are presented in the supporting information (SI, 2.2).

The fibroblast cells were seeded into a six-well culture plate with nutrient media and incubated for 24 h under the same conditions used for cell culture. In order to in vitro assay of DOX, cells were incubated with 1.0 mg mL−1 for each well and 1.0 cm2 of ENF scaffolds (Scheme 1e). Details are described in SI, 2.3.

The primary goal of this research was to introduce fluorescent nanofibrous hybrid device as a novel miniaturized platform in optical sensory and particularly for “on-the-spot” detection. In order to investigate its versatility in different settings, two different light detectors were set for the quantification of the targeted analyte: fiber optic probe to FL intensity analysis (Scheme 1c) and smartphone camera to image processing (Scheme 1d). The former case is promising for monitoring in laboratory-based setting where more accurate assay and lower detection limit is needed. While the latter case is applied for point-of-care assay without the need for a bulky analyzer such as a fluorimeter. The details of all fabrication steps of strip tests and setup of smart phone are schematized in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/PVA-ENFFs
ce:section.ce:para: NS-CDs were synthesized as the recognition probe for the proposed nanosensor by one pot and simple hydrothermal method with citric acid as the carbon source and rubeanic acid as a potent source for nitrogen and sulfur. This strategy aided us for the first time to obtain a high FL quantum yield (28.5%) NS-co-doped CDs with these precursors. The detail of quantum yield calculation is presented in SI, 3.1.

Fabrication of NS-CDs/ ENFFs were successfully realized by electrospinning the mixture of NS-CDs and PVA solution thanks to utilizing PVA as a support polymer owing to its advantageous properties such as biodegradability, biocompatibility, water solubility and electrospinibility[39], allowing uniform distribution of NS-CDs on the NF surface and further enhancing process of sensing. However, PVA-ENFs dissolve in water quickly; crosslinking post-treatment by GA vapor was employed to gain water-stable NFs.

TEM image of the as-prepared NS-CDs and their size distribution graph is shown in Fig. 1 a. The average diameter was determined to be 1.6 ± 0.2 nm. The morphologies and size distributions of the NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs are recorded by TEM and SEM. As depicted Fig. 1b, well-dispersed NS-CDs embedded in PVA-ENF is uniform over the fibrous film and no aggregation can be observed, indicating the effective protection of PVA molecular [11].

Evident facts were observed from SEM images that the PVA-ENFs with random orientation, a defect free morphology and the average diameters about 120 ± 5.15 nm are obtained as depicted in Fig. 1c. In addition, the cross-linked NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs reveal that an increase in diameters distribution (195 ± 10.5 nm) compared to the same without cross-linking (Fig. 1d). This could be attributed to create interconnected network after cross-linking at the cross-points [40]. Moreover, EDX elemental mapping of NS-CDs-embedded PVA-ENFs verifies that the spatial distribution of NS-CDs along the nanofiber is uniform, as depicted in Fig. 1e-i. This uniform allocation and configuration significantly overcomes the aggregation of NS-CDs in ENFs.

The successful integration of PVA-ENF with NS-CDs was further evidenced by the FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. S2a shows the FTIR spectra of NS-CDs (blue line), and the NS-CDs/ ENF (black line). The intense and slightly broad peak located at 3190 cm−1 and those at 1400 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 are ascribed to N-H, C-N and C = N stretching vibrations, respectively[41]. Several peaks also demonstrate presence of S atom in NS-CDs structure so that absorptions at 1095 cm−1 and 635 cm−1 refer to S = O and C-S stretching vibrations [42]. It can be concluded that N and S atoms were successfully doped at the surface of NS-CDs. Absorption band peaked at 1405 cm−1 also indicates the presence of O-H group. A typical band of all CDs, which is, signifies aromatic C = C stretch, is centered here at 1600 cm−1 [41,42]. After the fabrication of NS-CDs/ENF, the broaden band in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1 is appeared that might be induced by the formation of hydrogen bonding between plenty of functional NH2 groups of the CDs and hydroxyl groups of PVA[39,43].

Strong absorption peak at 1250 cm−1 is ascribed to one of the characteristic peaks of PVA, C − O stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups[39] (pink color arrows) along with the characteristic peaks of the as-prepared NS-CDs together (blue color dash lines) implying the incorporation of NS-CDs into PVA-ENFs. The FT-IR spectrum of both NS-CDs and NS-CDs/PVA ENFs demonstrates the presence of absorption peak at 2900 cm−1 related to the vibrations of aliphatic C–H.

The optical properties of the as-prepared NS-CDs/ENF have been explored by FL study. As evident from Fig. S2b, NS-CDs/ENF demonstrates a characteristic blue emission, which is implied to preserve the FL nature of NS-CDs after incorporating in PVA-ENF. There is also a slight blue shift in the FL emission of NS-CDs/ENFF when compared with the results for the NS-CDs in solution state. This might be due to two reasons: 1) the strong confinement of NS-CDs in a rigid nanofibrous matrix; 2) changes in polarity of the local dielectric environment of NS-CDs in solid state compared to solution [44]. Left and right insets show photograph of NS-CDs solution and NS-CDs/ENFF in the day light (top) and under UV light irradiation (λex = 360 nm) (bottom), respectively.

The photostability of NS-CDs was investigated by recording the FL emission intensity under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm for 1800 S. The stable signal output determined that the NS-CDs showed high photostability (Fig.S3a). Furthermore, the chemical stability of NS-CDs was also examined under various concentrations of sodium chloride. As can be seen from Fig. S3b, the FL intensity maintained almost constant in a broad salt concentration (0 to 120 mmol L-1), implying that NS-CDs hold good ionic strength. These results endow further amplification of NS-CDs in complex conditions.

It was necessary to optimize different sensing conditions in order to obtain the maximum analytical signal to noise ratio for DOX detection using the developed nanosensor. Details on the optimized conditions are described in SI, 3.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the DOX sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of NS-CDs in the sensor can be quenched by DOX addition. In Fig. 2 a, the continuous decreases in FL signal upon increasing different concentrations of DOX with a good linear correlation in two concentration ranges: 0.01–1.0 µM with a calibration equation of y = 0.265x + 1.07 and 1.0–15.0 µM with a calibration equation of y = 0.046x + 1.30, have been observed (Fig. 2b). F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities at 420.0 nm in the presence and absence of DOX. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as low as 5.40 nM based on 3σ/m, σ is the standard deviation of blank fluorescence signal and m is the slope of the related calibration curve.

Further, the assay was performed with a smartphone camera, in order to achieve as possible as to point-of-care settings. Before adding 4.0 µL of DOX solution, all the detection circular areas in ENFFAD strip exhibited bright intense and comparable blue FL features. The first one has been taken as a blank signal (the detection area containing 0 µM DOX). As evident from the Fig. 2c, the blue color is gradually weakened with the increase of DOX concentration, enabling the visualized detection by naked eye. Upon exposure of 50.0 µM DOX, the FL intensity of NS-CDs/ENFF totally disappeared (Turn off), while upon adding of 0.5 µM, accepted response has been noticed following the taken of images. In this sense, an Apple iPhone 7 smartphone was used to take a picture after 10 min (drying time). The pictures were analyzed by Image J software to transform the FL intensity to numerical value from 0 to 255 (where 0 is dark and 255 is maximum brightness). The calibration plot is observed in Fig. 2d, which shows a good linear correlation in two concentration ranges: 0.50–10.0 µM with a calibration equation of y = 3.10 x + 7.66 and 10.0–30.0 µM with a calibration equation of y = 1.984x + 20.99. The calculated LOD (3σ/m) was 320.0 nM.

Table S1 shows that the analytical performance of our proposed ENFFAD was comparable to the fluorescence detection of DOX based on different kinds of probes. Besides, it has the potential to detect DOX by the naked eye without using expensive and bulky fluorimeter analyzer to record signals and toxic organic solvents or reagent. Another advantages that make it be a novel and unique affordable test are consumption of very small volumes of sample containing analyte and real applicability in resources-limited settings.

To examine further selectivity of the NS-CD as a DOX selective recognition probe, its FL quenching response in the presence of some possible interfering agents to DOX detection such as Ascorbic Acid (AA), Glucose (Glu), Uric Acid (UA), Caffeine (CA), Dopamine (DA), L-Lysine (Lys), L-Cysteine (Cys), and some common ions like Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+ , Ca2+ and Zn2+ were investigated. The experiments were performed under the same condition as mentioned above. As expected, there was no significant change observed in the FL intensity of the NS-CDs/DOX thereby do not interfere with DOX sensing as illustrated in Fig. 3 a.

We also examined the selectivity of NS-CDs/ENFF by employing a series of mentioned materials and DOX (50.0 µM), similar to the DOX detection procedure (Fig. 3b). As can be seen from the image, dramatic FL quenching has been obtained upon the addition of DOX, whereas no visible changes were observed by naked eye under UV light. This good selectivity may be attributed to strong IFE between the incorporated NS-CDs into the developed senor and DOX.
As discussed earlier, and presented in Fig. 2E, the FL emission of NS-CDs solution was found to be selectively quenched by DOX.

In order to explore the exact quenching mechanism of the as-prepared NS-CDs due to DOX, some studies were performed. A large spectral overlap was found between the broad absorption band of DOX and the FL excitation/emission spectra of the NS-CDs (see Fig. 4 a). Firstly, making it certain that FL quenching of NS-CDs might be due to the IFE or FRET process, two possible mechanism result in energy transfer[25,45] , time resolved decay time tests were performed.

Since no change of the life times for NS-CDs was observed before and after adding 50.0 µM DOX (Fig. 4b), suggesting no FRET process does occur between DOX and NS-CDs and IFE was considered to be as a primary case of the FL quenching [25,45].

In order to confirm the scenario, by taking into account the cuvette geometry (Fig. 4c, inset) and absorption characteristics of the DOX and NS-CDs solutions, the IFE is corrected with the equation (1) [25,46]:
(1)
F
c
o
r
r
F
O
b
s
d
=
2.3
d
A
e
x
1 -
10
-
d
A
e
x
∙
s
A
e
m
10
g
A
e
m
1 -
10
-
s
A
e
m

where, Fobsd is the observed FL intensity and Fcor is the corrected FL intensity by removing IFE from Fobsd; Aem and Aex refer to the absorbance at maximum emission wavelength (λem=440 nm) and the excitation wavelength of NS-CDs (λex=340 nm), respectively. Table S2 summarizes the calculated results related to the correction factor (CF) for IFE of each different concentration of DOX into the NS-CDs solution. In Fig. 4c, it was shown that the suppressed efficiency of totally observed (Eobsd) and corrected (Ecor) fluorescence of NS-CDs, after removing IFE, could reach as high as 70%, which confirms the quenching efficiency mainly derived from IFE. However, the rest of FL quenching of NS-CDs caused by DOX might be attributed to molecular interactions like electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding between them. More interestingly, DOX has a molar extinction coefficient as high as 11500 M−1 cm−1 leads to better IFE efficiency [47].Taken all, IFE mechanism can explain the selectivity of NS-CDs toward DOX.

For investigating the applicability of the proposed sensor in the detection of DOX in real complex samples, the solid-state sensor was evaluated. Pre-treated serum samples were spiked with different DOX concentrations and analyzed by the proposed method (Table 1 ). The recovery range which is expressing the reliability and accuracy of the sensing method was between 91.25% − 107.0%. The relative standard deviation (RSD) values were lower than 3.2%, revealing the high precision of this method. The above results indicate that the NS-CDs/ENFFs are desire as a solid-state sensing strategy for targeted analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular imaging and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: At first we evaluated the safety of NS-CDs on human fibroblast cell line using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S6, the NS-CDs did not show significant cytotoxic effect on the cells even at higher dose (up to 1.0 mg mL−1) after 24 h incubation. Moreover, if NS-CDs/PVA-ENF scaffold is going to be used for bioimaging, it is mandatory to investigate its cytotoxic effect. Therefore, the relative viability in the complex matrices were evaluated in the fibroblast cell line using MTT assay. For this reason, 2 × 105 cells /well were seeded. After 24 h, the cells were exposed with NS-CDs/PVA-ENF scaffolds for 24–72 h. The free NS-CDs and bare PVA- ENF scaffolds were used as controls under the same condition. Fig. 5 a reveals that: both bare PVA-ENF and NS-CDs/PVA-ENF scaffolds do not appear significant cytotoxic effect in the cell line; so 1 cm2 was used for biomaging; at every culture time, the cell growth on NS-CDs/PVA ENFs is better than that on untreated cells, indicating safety of ENF scaffolds. NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs showed proliferative and growth activity on fibroblast cells significantly in comparison to both PVA ENFs and free NS-CDs after 48 h cell incubation. It is expected that, the synergistic effects of two main factors give rise to the enhancement of the cell proliferation via NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs; i) Since PVA is a polymer with no adhesive and low protein adsorption property, it may prevent the binding of extracellular matrix (ECM) to support cell spreading and attachment [48,49]. ii) Both various functional groups on the surface and interior of fibrous matrix as a result of incorporating of NS-CDs and higher cell surface interaction due to high surface area property of nanofibers, as well stimulate the natural ECM. All result in an enhanced interaction between nanofibrous and cells, further may lead to in vitro well cell-matrix adhesion.

The cellular uptake of the NS-CDs and NS-CDs/PVA-ENF scaffold as well as their impact on cell morphology were then investigated with FL microscope. Fig. 5 b-e displays the FL images (at bright field and excitation wavelength of 405 nm) of the human fibroblast cells after incubation with the NS-CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) and NS-CDs/PVA-ENF scaffolds (1.0 mg mL−1 CDs-incorporated in 1.0 cm2 ENF scaffold) for 2 h, respectively. As it can be seen in Fig. 5c and e, both the free NS-CDs and NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs are internalized into the cytoplasm because of excellent biocompatibility while the FL intensity of NS-CDs/PVA-ENF-treated cells (Fig. 5c) is much higher as compared to that of free NS-CDs-treated cells (Fig. 5e). The reason could be attribute to the higher cellular uptake efficiency of ENF through endocytosis[50].

Moreover, the morphologies in the cells treated with NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs was notably changed, whereas the cells incubated with NS-CDs remains unchanged and almost same towards blank control group (Fig. 5 corresponded bright field images). These results let us to make sure that NSCDs/PVA-ENFs can support proliferation of fibroblast cells, leading to cell morphological change triggered by the cell-NF scaffold interaction.

The following step was to verify the practicability of NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs for living cell imaging of DOX. First, the cells were incubated with NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 6 b and e, strong blue and green fluorescence is clearly exhibited inside cells by excitation at 405 and 488 nm, respectively. This multicolor emission provides a variety to select the wavelength for observation, making the probe as a bio-labeling and cellular imaging agent [51]. Upon addition of 5.0 µM DOX to the cells, the FL emission diminished quickly (Fig. 6c and f), due to being potent of NS-CDs/PVA ENFFs for specific recognition of DOX within cells. As we expected, the obtained results were similar to what we have observed for DOX detection in PB solution by NS-CDs/PVA-ENFFs probe. Taken together, all of the above experiments confirmed that NS-CDs/PVA-ENFs is a promising tool not only to develop a non-toxic bioimaging probe for more complex biological samples, but also it can be further potentially used as a versatile scaffolds for biomedical applications.

A new disposable and ready-to–use device was developed using fluorescent ENF film-based optical sensor strategy. The detection principle is based on the selective quenching FL of NS-CDs upon the addition of DOX through inner filter effect mechanism and observing the color changes by a mobile phone camera or spectrofluorimeter. The sensor has a high potential for detecting DOX concentration in a sensitive and reliable way in human serum samples.

However, the proposed device provides minimum handling of the sample, reduces required sample volume, allows point-of-care sensing in one step and can be used for direct TDM of DOX in biofluids. Due to the outstanding features of ENF film as sensing platform along with nontoxicity and simplicity in the fabrication of ENFFAD, the probe can be applied as a biocompatible, biodegradable and cost efficient tool in sensing and biomedical fields.
This study is financially supported by the Research Councils of Bu-Ali Sina University.

Niloufar Amin: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Abbas Afkhami: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Leila Hosseinzadeh: Validated & biological data, Writing - review & editing. Ali Amin: Formal analysis, Methodology. Tayyebeh Madrakian: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119944.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
